ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to chapati/phulka; A00LC (flatbread, whole wheat) used as closest parent.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard phulka chapati weighs approximately 40 g and is typically served as 2–3 pieces per meal alongside dal, curry, or vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Chapati, without fat — Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Phulka is the puffed, fat-free variant of chapati. ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods lists chapati without fat, which is the standard reference for phulka. Values per 100 g edible portion used directly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Chapati, whole wheat
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 52 for whole wheat chapati is reported in Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008 (Table of GI values). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (52 × 24.6 g available carbohydrate per 40 g serving) / 100 = 12.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~12 mg per 100 g) when prepared without added salt
hypertension.guidance: Chapati (phulka) is suitable for individuals with hypertension as it is naturally very low in sodium; patients should avoid adding salt during preparation and monitor accompaniments such as pickles or chutneys that may be high in sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (GI 52) and significant carbohydrate content (~62 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Whole wheat chapati has a low-to-moderate GI of 52 and provides dietary fibre (~3.9 g per 100 g) which slows glucose absorption; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 1–2 phulkas per meal and pair with high-fibre vegetables or legumes to further blunt postprandial glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and whole grain fibre content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chapati (phulka) prepared without added fat is low in saturated fat (0.4 g per 100 g) and contains no cholesterol, making it a heart-friendly staple; the whole wheat fibre content supports LDL cholesterol reduction when consumed as part of a balanced diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chapati (Phulka) is a traditional unleavened Indian flatbread made from whole wheat flour (atta), water, and a pinch of salt. The dough is kneaded until smooth, divided into small balls, and rolled into thin discs approximately 15–18 cm in diameter. The phulka variant is cooked first on a dry tawa (cast-iron griddle) until light brown spots appear, then held directly over an open flame or placed on a wire rack above the burner, causing it to puff up dramatically with steam — this puffing is the defining characteristic of a phulka. The result is a soft, slightly chewy flatbread with a mild, nutty whole wheat flavour and a thin, lightly charred exterior. Phulka is lighter and smaller than a standard chapati and contains no added fat, distinguishing it from paratha or roti cooked with ghee or oil. It is a staple across North and South Indian cuisine and is widely served in Indian restaurants in Singapore, including at establishments such as Maharani Table. Nutritionally, phulka provides approximately 297 kcal per 100 g, with 9 g protein, 1.9 g fat, and 61.6 g carbohydrate, along with 3.9 g dietary fibre from the whole wheat. Its glycemic index of 52 places it in the low-GI category, making it a more favourable carbohydrate choice compared to white rice or refined bread. It is naturally vegan, halal, and dairy-free. Clinically, it is suitable for individuals managing hypertension due to its very low sodium content, and its whole grain fibre supports cardiovascular health and glycaemic control in Type 2 Diabetes when portion size is managed.
common_user_queries: Is chapati the same as phulka?
How many calories in a chapati?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods for chapati without fat, which is the canonical Indian nutrition reference for this exact food. GI value of 52 sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed table for whole wheat chapati. Ontology codes verified against FoodOn and SNOMED CT.
notes: Phulka is the fat-free, puffed variant of chapati cooked over direct flame; ICMR-NIN chapati-without-fat entry is the correct reference. Trans fat is 0.0 (confirmed zero — whole wheat flour contains negligible industrial trans fats; confirmed per ICMR-NIN source). Cholesterol is 0.0 (confirmed zero — plant-based food with no animal-derived fat). Vitamin C is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no vitamin C present in cooked whole wheat flatbread per ICMR-NIN). Vitamin B12 is 0.0 (confirmed zero — plant-based food; no B12 naturally present). Vitamin A (mcg RAE) is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this entry. Vitamin B6 is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this entry. Folate (mcg DFE) is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source in DFE units. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this entry. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this entry. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this entry. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI 52 × available carbohydrate 24.6 g per 40 g serving) / 100 = 12.8. Available carbohydrate = total carbohydrate 61.6 g per 100 g × 0.40 serving = 24.64 g. Cuisine field set to Indian (origin) rather than Singapore (serving context) to reflect true culinary origin; field survey data context preserved in source and restaurant metadata.
